If 2 [[REDESIGNED]] reminder tokens are on the grimoire, swap the character tokens of those two players. Wake each player separately and show them the 'You are' card, then their new character token.
examples: Nyla and Bruce are the Actor and Standard Bearer. At night, the Architect chooses Nyla. Nyla and Bruce's character tokens are swapped. They are woken separately and shown their new character token.
Jack is Cleopatra. At night, the Architect chooses Jack. Jack's character token is replaced with the Caesar token. Jack is woken and shown that they are now Caesar.
quote: "I found Rome a city of bricks and left it the city of marbles." — Caesar Augustus 63 B.C.
otherNightReminder: The Architect points to a player. Choose which ability will effect the chosen player. Treat them accordingly (not-in-play character or character swap). Wake affected players individually. Show 'You are', then their new character token.

name: Sibyl
ability: Each day, after the 1st execution, you may publicly choose a dead player: they may nominate. If the majority of the dead and yourself agree, they are executed.
creator: Alex S.
lede: The Sibyl consults with the dead, letting them nominate and execute players.
howToRun: After the first execution each day, if the Sibyl publicly chooses a dead player, that dead player may nominate. If the majority of the dead and the Sibyl agree, they are executed.
examples: Murphy dies during the night. Jamie, the good Sibyl travels in. The next day Jamie, after the 1st execution, chooses Murphy. That day Murphy is able to nominate and does so. Talia will receive new information tonight as their 'Sculpture' Murphy nominated a new player.

Sophia is the Glykon. On the first day Sophia was executed and dies. The Sibyl picks Sophia. Sophia nominates a good player, but neither Sophia nor the Sibyl decide to vote on it. The good player is not executed nor are they affected by the Glykon's ability, as Sophia was dead when they nominated.
tips: The majority required is equal to half the number of dead players and the Sibyl. The Sibyl themself does not have to agree if the votes from the dead reach the majority.
Note that the Mortician will gain information from every execution, so is a more powerful role than normal when the Sibyl is in play.
quote: "To such a degree will I be changed that I will be visible to no one; but I will be recognized by my voice. And with my voice, to you, I give a second chance."

name: High Priest (Pontifex Maximus)
ability: Each day, publicly choose a unique living player to bless: if a majority of players agree, something good happens to them.
creator: Alex S.
lede: The High Priest can bless players, bringing good fortune to that player and their team.
howToRun: Each day, if the High Priest chooses to bless a player, announce to the town that there will be a vote. Dead players may vote and do not require a ghost vote to do so. If a majority voted place the [[BLESSED]] reminder token next to the chosen player, otherwise place the [[FAILED]] reminder token. That player cannot be selected again by the High Priest. The effect of the blessing is up to the discretion of the storyteller — inherently it should help the player and/or their team.
If the High Priest chooses a player that they have already blessed or tried to bless, ask the High Priest to choose a different player.
examples: Antonio is a Bad Omen who believes they are the Trumpeter. During the day the High Priest chooses to bless Antonio. The town vote and a majority is met. To bless Antonio, the Storyteller removes the BAD OMEN reminder token, so that Antonio will receive true information.

Anne is the Temptress. During the day the High Priest chooses to bless Anne. The town vote and a majority is achieved. After being blessed, Anne's Demon is executed and dies, however the game continues. The Storyteller wakes Anne and shows her the Cleopatra token — Anne is the new Demon.
tips: There are two High Priest variants available. Only one type of High Priest can be in a game of Fall of Rome.

The Pontifex Maximus leads to a more chaotic and random version of Fall of Rome. The Storyteller can freely use the High Priest to provide good or evil players, when blessed, a large range of 'good' things.
The Pontiff provides for a more solvable, information-centric contribution.
quote: "To ignore the signs of the gods is itself to deny their very existence." — Cicero 106 B.C.
otherNightReminder: If the High Priest successfully blessed a player today, something good happens to that player and/or their team.

name: High Priest (Pontiff)
ability: Each day, publicly choose a unique living player to bless: if a majority of players agree, tomorrow they may learn a statement. Tonight, choose if it's true.
creator: Alex S.
lede: The High Priest can bless players, bringing good fortune to that player and their team.
howToRun: Each day, if the High Priest chooses to bless a player, announce to the town that there will be a vote. If a majority voted place the [[BLESSED]] reminder token next to the chosen player, otherwise place the [[FAILED]] reminder token. That player cannot be selected again.

Each night, if the High Priest successfully blessed a player today, wake the High Priest. The High Priest chooses whether the statement is true tomorrow with a hand signal (true 'thumbs up', false 'thumbs down').
tips: There are two High Priest variants available. Only one type of High Priest can be in a game of Fall of Rome.
The Pontiff provides for a more solvable, information-centric contribution, with the added ability for the High Priest to choose whether the blessed players receive true or false information.
quote: "Deny everything, burn the records, say these are lies, anything not to recognise that gods look after our human affairs." — Cicero 106 B.C.
otherNightReminder: If the High Priest successfully blessed a player today, wake the High Priest. The High Priest chooses whether the statement is true tomorrow with a hand signal (true 'thumbs up', false 'thumbs down').

name: Emperor
ability: Each day, choose the 1st execution's outcome. If you choose to protect today's execution: they survive. Otherwise, tonight you learn their alignment.
creator: Alex S.
lede: The Emperor decides the outcome of the executions — with a thumbs up or a thumbs down.
howToRun: Each day, before the execution goes through ask the Emperor whether they will overturn the execution (thumbs up), or go through with the execution (thumbs down). If they overturn the execution the player executed does not die. The game continues and everyone goes to sleep.

Each night, if the Emperor went through with today's execution (thumbs down), wake the Emperor. They learn the player's alignment. If good, tell them 'good' (thumbs up) otherwise tell them 'evil' (thumbs down).
examples: Patrizia is a Bad Omen who believes they are the Standard Bearer. During the day they are nominated and executed. Before the execution goes through the Storyteller calls upon the Emperor. The Emperor shows a thumbs down. Patrizia is executed and dies. That night, the Emperor learns that Patrizia is evil as they are falsely registering as a Bad Omen.
Gary is Crassus. During the day they are nominated and executed. The Emperor decides to save Gary by showing a thumbs up. Gary is executed and does not die, and the game continues.
quote: "Which death is preferable to every other? The unexpected."
otherNightReminder: If a player was executed today and the outcome was not overturned by the Emperor: Show the hand signal (thumbs down 'evil', thumbs up 'good') for the players alignment.

name: "I Am Spartacus!"
ability: Each day, up to half the living players (rounded up & Travellers don't count) may publicly claim to be Spartacus.
creator: Alex S.
lede: Living players may publicly stake their claim as Spartacus each day.
howToRun: At the start of the day, remove all [[I AM SPARTACUS!]] reminder tokens, if any.
During the day, if a player publicly claims to be Spartacus place the [[I AM SPARTACUS!]] reminder token next to that player.
Only up to half the living players (rounded up and not counting Travellers) may publicly claim to be Spartacus.
Players may ask the Storyteller during the day who has publicly claimed to be Spartacus so far. This is public information that the Storyteller can divulge.
examples: Mack is Spartacus. During the day, Mack publicly claims to be Spartacus. The Storyteller places the I AM SPARTACUS! reminder token next to Spartacus. Spartacus' ability condition is met, and they might register as a Townsfolk.

Gary is Crassus. During the day, Gary publicly claims to be Spartacus. The Storyteller places the I AM SPARTACUS! reminder token next to Crassus. Crassus' ability condition is met — if they die today or tonight, a living evil player becomes Crassus.
tips: Mechanical Spartacus claims are a large part of the game. The Storyteller should emphasise this at the start of the game, and reiterate that they are happy to state who has claimed Spartacus each day when asked. The Storyteller should also inform players how many mechanical claims are left.
Consider using physical markers visible in the Town Square to show who has claimed Spartacus. Flags and hats are a fun way to show who has claimed Spartacus today.
Make clear to the players that you will not be able to confirm Spartacus claims from previous days, only the current day.
quote: "The ego cuts deeper than the flesh." — Spartacus c. 103 B.C.

name: Bard
ability: Each day, publicly choose any living players: recently chosen Townsfolk are drunk. Each night*, learn how many are drunk this way.
creator: Taiyi
lede: The Bard can get Townsfolk drunk to learn their numbers.
summaryBullets: The Bard learns the number of "Drunk" tokens placed at the moment the information is revealed to them.
The drunkenness caused by the Bard lasts from the moment they publicly select a player until the next Bard's public selection.

Aim to have the Bard make only one public selection per day; repeatedly adjusting drunk tokens is cumbersome. The Bard cannot retract a player they have already selected, but if necessary, they may supplement their selection with additional players.
If a Townsfolk drunkened by the Bard becomes a non-townsfolk role, that player immediately regains sobriety (drunkenness is removed).
The Bard may only select living players during their choice. If a Townsfolk they select dies during the night, it does not affect the Bard's determination that they are drunk.
howToRun: At any point during the day, the Bard may publicly select any living player. After selection, place a [[DRUNK]] token next to the chosen townsfolk's token and remove any previously placed [[DRUNK]] tokens.
These players become drunk from this point onward until the Bard makes another public selection.
During the night phase, wake the Bard and show them the number of "Drunk" tokens currently in play.
examples: On the first day, the Bard publicly chooses three players, including two Townsfolk. The two Townsfolk players immediately become drunk. That night, the Bard wakes and learns "2".

On the first day, the Bard publicly chooses three players, including two Townsfolk. The Townsfolk immediately become drunk. That night, the Demon kills the Bard. The 2 Townsfolk become sober and their abilities work normally that night.
On the first day, the Bard chooses the Gambler, who then becomes drunk. That night the Pit-Hag turns the Gambler into the Klutz. As the Klutz is no longer a Townsfolk, they become sober.
tips: Before publicly declaring your choice, it's best to communicate with the player you intend to select to avoid drunkening crucial Townsfolk roles. Of course, you may choose not to do so, but you risk being executed outright.
If you wish to select a player with a daytime ability like the Artist, it's best to let them activate their ability first. Otherwise, your selection may cause them to become drunk immediately.
Encourage other players to claim they are the Bard. This makes it difficult for the Demon to identify the real Bard among you early on.
You may choose only Townsfolk who have already used their abilities. This both verifies their Townsfolk status and avoids drunkening crucial Townsfolk.
You can choose only Outsiders. If you learn they are Townsfolk, your information is definitely wrong.

Before selecting too many players, think carefully. Getting a large number of Townsfolk drunk might not be a good idea for the good team, and it's hard to tell who's a townsfolk and who isn't in a crowd of players.
quote: Souls touched by my fingertips shall plunge into dreams. While you stagger in alcohol's embrace, I count the vibrating silk strings... Three? Five? These numbers reveal how many honest souls expose lies' shapes within sweet slumber.
otherNightReminder: Wake the Bard. Show them the number of DRUNK tokens currently in play.

name: Archlich
ability: Each night*, choose a player: they die. The 1st time you choose each dead player, they choose 2 players: chosen non-demons die.
creator: Taiyi
lede: The Archlich can infuse corpses with energy, granting them tremendous power. The Archlich may give up their own kill, allowing a corpse to act in their place — killing 2 players that night. However, the corpse controls who dies, and each corpse can only be used once.
howToRun: Each night except the first, wake the Archlich. Have the Archlich point to any player. Put the Archlich to sleep.
If they chose a living player, mark that player with the [[DEAD]] reminder token.

If they chose a dead player who does not already have a [[TRIGGERED]] reminder token: wake that dead player and show them in sequence the THIS ABILITY AFFECTS YOU reminder token and the Archlich character token. Place the [[TRIGGERED]] reminder token next to that player's character token. Have them choose 2 players. Mark whichever of those 2 players is not the Demon with the [[DEAD]] reminder token.
At dawn, announce which players died during the night.
Choosing a dead Traveller does not trigger the Archlich's ability. If the Archlich chooses a dead Traveller, inform them that this does not activate their ability.
callout: Each player can trigger the Archlich's ability at most once. The [[TRIGGERED]] token is never removed for any reason. All Archliches and players with the Archlich's ability share a single pool of already-triggered dead players — even if the original Archlich dies or is replaced.
examples: On the second night, the Archlich chooses the living Poisoner. The Poisoner dies. The following night, the Archlich chooses the dead Poisoner. The Poisoner chooses the Noble and the Fortune Teller. The Noble and the Fortune Teller die.

On the second night, the Archlich chooses the dead Poisoner. The Poisoner chooses the Noble and the Fortune Teller. They die. The following day, the Poisoner publicly nominates the Hell's Castaway. The Hell's Castaway and the Archlich swap characters, becoming the new Archlich. That night, the new Archlich chooses the dead Poisoner — nobody dies. Each player can only trigger the Archlich's ability once, regardless of which Archlich does the choosing.

On the first day, the Noble dies by execution. That night, the Archlich chooses the dead Noble. The Noble chooses the Poisoner and the Archlich. The Poisoner dies. At dawn, the Noble immediately announces they were chosen by the Archlich.

On the first day, the Noble dies by execution. That night, the Archlich chooses the dead Noble. The Noble chooses 2 dead players. Nobody dies. The following night, the Archlich chooses the dead Noble again — the Noble is not woken, as each player can only trigger the ability once.
tips: As the Archlich, you must learn to make use of corpses. If you only ever choose living players, you are playing as a perfectly ordinary Demon with no special ability. Every dead player is a resource — think carefully about how to use the dead to your advantage.
Choosing a dead evil player is a smart move. They will kill in your interests and will not voluntarily reveal your existence — but the number of dead evil players is always limited.

Dead good players are an inexhaustible supply. However, the good team will learn you exist when a dead good player announces they were chosen. They may also choose targets you'd rather they didn't — including you. If they choose you and you don't die, they will be almost certain you are the Demon.
quote: I heard you. The resentment in your soul toward death, the hunger for power. The world fears you — and I admire you. Accept this gift. Your corpse will become my first masterpiece, and your will shall become the law by which all living things are judged. Breathe deep this sweet terror... it will be the first meal of your new life.
otherNightReminder: The Archlich points to a player. If living: that player dies. If dead and no TRIGGERED token: wake that dead player, show THIS ABILITY AFFECTS YOU and the Archlich token, place TRIGGERED. They choose 2 players — whichever is not the Demon dies.
fighting: If multiple deaths seem Archlich-caused but no player has claimed to have been chosen, at least one previously dead player must be evil — most likely one who died by execution.
If a player claims they were chosen by the Archlich and the living player they chose did not die, that player who didn't die is almost certainly the Demon.

Do not blindly trust dead players who claim they were chosen by the Archlich. Evil players can use this tactic to steer the good team into incorrect assumptions or lead the town to execute a good player.

If you are a dead good player woken by the Archlich, choose carefully. You could pick 2 dead players to minimise further deaths, or choose players you believe to be evil to reduce the evil team's numbers. The choice is yours.
If you were previously woken by the Archlich and the living player you chose did not die, that player is almost certainly the Demon. Announce this publicly.

name: Ban Xian
ability: If you are chosen by a character’s ability at night, an evil player will be targeted instead.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: That man is always covered in grime or rambling incoherently. I can't get a grip on him.
lede: The Ban Xian redirects potential threats to evil players.
summaryBullets: At night, if a player chooses the Ban Xian with their ability, the ability will instead resolve as if they chose an evil player of the Storyteller's choice.
Players will not be told that their ability did not affect the intended target, nor will the Ban Xian be told that their ability has activated.
The Ban Xian only redirects abilities that require the player to actively choose a target. Passive abilities still affect the Ban Xian as usual.
The Storyteller can choose to redirect the ability to an otherwise invalid target, such as a dead evil player for the Snake Charmer.
An ability redirected by the Ban Xian cannot be redirected further.
The Ban Xian ability can only be activated at night. If the Ban Xian is chosen by an ability during the day, that ability is not redirected.
howToRun: Each time the Ban Xian player is chosen by a character's ability at night, choose an evil player to redirect the ability towards. Resolve the ability as if that player was chosen (and was a legitimate choice).
examples: The Fortune Teller chooses the Ban Xian and the Scarlet Woman and learns "yes", since the Imp is targeted instead.

On the first night, the Devil's Advocate protects themself. On the second night, the Devil's Advocate chooses the Ban Xian, and the Storyteller decides to redirect the protection to the Devil's Advocate, making the Devil's Advocate able to survive execution for the second consecutive day. On the third night, the Devil's Advocate chooses themself, as their previous choice was the Ban Xian.
The Psychopath chooses to attack the Ban Xian during the day. The Ban Xian dies, since their ability does not function during the day.
tips: Claim a role that evil players are likely to visit — if the evil team targets you, they only help you. Bluffing as a Fortune Teller or Undertaker can bait valuable night actions into backfiring.
You may never know if your ability triggers, but you can infer it: if an evil player claims to have gotten info on you, their info was likely corrupted by your redirect.
Be wary of the Assassin — their kill bypasses your ability entirely and will kill you outright.
Your ability only protects against abilities that involve choosing. Players poisoning you through other mechanics or abilities that don't make a choice bypass you entirely.

jinxes.reason: If a Riot player nominates the Ban Xian on day 3, another evil player dies instead of the Ban Xian, and nominates next. If the nominator is the only living evil player, the Ban Xian dies as normal.
jinxes.name: Riot

jinxes.reason: The Kazali can choose that the Ban Xian player is one of their evil Minions.
jinxes.name: Kazali

jinxes.reason: The Lleech’s poisoning or host choice cannot be redirected.
jinxes.name: Lleech

callout: If the Assassin attacks the Ban Xian, the Ban Xian dies without redirecting the Assassin's choice to an evil player.

name: Face Changer
ability: If you are mad that you are a good character: tonight, you might gain their ability until dusk (once per character).
creator: Gstone Games
quote: One minute of glory on stage, ten years of hard work off stage.
lede: The Face Changer is adept at assuming many different roles.
summaryBullets: If the Face Changer is "mad" that they are a certain good character, they might be able to gain that character's ability that night.
If the Face Changer gains an ability, they keep it until dusk.
The Face Changer cannot gain the same character's ability more than once.
Only the Face Changer's madness from the previous day is taken into account when deciding whether they gain an ability.

Some characters' abilities may activate at night depending on the player's actions during the day. If the Face Changer is "mad" that they are such a character, they need to make the appropriate actions for the ability to have any effect.
howToRun: At night, if you believe that the Face Changer has been "mad" that they are a good character whose ability they have not already gained, they gain that ability and wake when that character would normally wake. They have this ability until dusk.
examples: On the first day, the Face Changer is "mad" that they are the Fortune Teller. That night, the Face Changer is woken to choose two players. They choose the Imp and the Preacher, and learn "yes". On the second day, the Face Changer convinces the town that they are actually the Seamstress. The following night, they wake to choose the Preacher and the Sage, and learn "yes". On the third day, the Face Changer claims to be the Dreamer, but does not provide sufficient explanation as to why they claimed a different character yesterday. The Storyteller decides not to give them the Dreamer ability. That night, the Face Changer does not wake.

On the first three days, the Face Changer is "mad" that they are the Steward. They gain the Steward's ability on the second night and learn a good player, but gain nothing on the third and fourth nights. On the fourth day, the Face Changer is "mad" that they are the Knight. The fifth night, the Face Changer is woken and shown two players that are not the Demon.
tips: Plan your character arc. Each claim is a one-time opportunity — pick characters with abilities that help your current situation most.
The more believable your performance, the more likely you are to be granted the ability. Give your town a compelling narrative for why you're switching characters.
Be aware that convincingly claiming evil characters won't help you — only good characters' abilities can be gained.
Consider claiming characters whose abilities benefit from you being alive and active, since your power only lasts until the next dusk.
callout: When the Face Changer is "mad" about being a new good character, they need to be able to explain why they claimed to be a different character on previous days. Generally, a Face Changer might succeed in gaining one or two new abilities during a game, but you as the Storyteller are the final judge.

name: Firewatcher
ability: Each night*, guess your number of steps to the 1st to die tonight: if correct, you might die tonight, but no one else.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Have you not heard of the prohibition on candles? This could burn the village down in this weather!
lede: The Firewatcher keeps an eye out on areas at risk at night.
summaryBullets: If the first player to die at night is exactly as many steps from the Firewatcher as the Firewatcher guessed, that player will not die tonight.
If the Firewatcher successfully prevents the death of a player, no other player except the Firewatcher can die that night.
The Firewatcher does not protect themself, and may in fact die to their own ability if they successfully protect somebody else.
The Firewatcher's number takes into account all players, not just all living players. If both neighbors are dead, a guess of "1" will have no effect.
The Firewatcher can guess "0", choosing themself. If the Firewatcher is then the first to die that night, they die as normal, but nobody else may die that night.
If the first player to die at night is not sitting the guessed number of steps away, or if nobody dies, the Firewatcher's ability does not activate.
howToRun: Each night except the first, wake the Firewatcher. They hold up a number of fingers (0, 1, 2 etc.) indicating the distance in players from themself to the players they want to protect. Mark both players that distance away with the [[PROTECTED]] reminder tokens. Put the Firewatcher to sleep.

If a player marked with a [[PROTECTED]] token would then be the first player to die that night, place the [[PROTECTED]] token in the center of the Grimoire. No player except for the Firewatcher can die tonight. If the Firewatcher survives the night, decide if they should die to their own ability or not: if yes, mark them with the [[DEAD]] reminder token. At dawn, remove all [[PROTECTED]] reminders and declare if any players died at night, without saying why or how they died or survived.
If a player not marked with a [[PROTECTED]] reminder token is the first to die at night, remove all [[PROTECTED]] reminder tokens and resolve the death and the rest of the night normally.
examples: The Firewatcher guesses "2". Tonight, the Demon attacks a player 2 steps away from the Firewatcher, and then the Godfather attacks a player 5 steps away. The Demon is the first character to cause a death tonight, so the Firewatcher's ability activates, and neither the Demon nor the Godfather succeed in killing their targets. Towards the end of the night, the Storyteller decides that the Firewatcher dies.

The Firewatcher guesses "2". Tonight, the Po chooses no-one. The Assassin chooses to kill a player 2 steps away from the Firewatcher. That player dies, and the Firewatcher stays alive, as they did not manage to protect anybody.

The Firewatcher guesses "2". Tonight, the Demon attacks a player 3 steps away, and the Godfather attacks a player 2 steps away. Since the Demon attacked before the Godfather, both targeted players die, and the Firewatcher stays alive.
tips: Pay attention to who is sitting near you. Strong info roles seated close make tempting Demon targets — guessing short distances is often wise.
Guessing 0 is a bold gambit — if the Demon is targeting you, you save everyone else. But you'll almost certainly die.
Dead players still count for distance calculations. Keep track of the seat order carefully as players die — your effective coverage shrinks each night.
If your ability doesn't trigger for several nights, it may mean the Demon is attacking far from you, or no one is dying. Use this as information about where threats might be coming from.
otherNightReminder: The Firewatcher holds up a number of fingers (0, 1, 2 etc.). :reminder: :reminder:

jinxes.reason: If the Leviathan is in play, the Firewatcher-protected players are safe from all evil abilities.
jinxes.name: Leviathan

jinxes.reason: If the Riot is in play, the Firewatcher-protected players are safe from all evil abilities.
jinxes.name: Riot

callout: The Brocadier cannot protect the Firewatcher from dying to its own ability, as the Firewatcher dying prevents the Brocadier from dying to their ability.

name: Waiter
ability: You start knowing 2 good players: 1 is drunk, even if you are dead.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Welcome back, sir! The usual, I assume?
lede: The Waiter knows their regulars and makes sure they have plenty to drink.
summaryBullets: On their first night, the Waiter learns two players that are good at that point.
One of the players the Waiter learns is drunk the whole game, even if the Waiter dies.
If created mid-game, the Waiter learns their information that night, and one of the players they know is drunk from then on.
If a dead player becomes the Waiter, they do not learn two good players, but 1 good player is drunk from then on.
howToRun: While preparing the first night, put the Waiter's [[KNOWN]] token by any good player token, and put the Waiter's [[DRUNK]] token by any other good player token.

During the first night, wake the Waiter and point to the players marked [[KNOWN]] and [[DRUNK]]. Put the Waiter to sleep. The player marked [[DRUNK]] will remain drunk for as long as the Waiter is in play.
examples: The Waiter learns the Snake Charmer and the Philosopher. The Philosopher is drunk. Later, the Snake Charmer chooses the Demon and becomes the evil Demon, but the Waiter still thinks that they are good.
The Pacifist is turned into the Waiter. They learn the Alchemist and the Lycanthrope, who is made drunk. Later, the Lycanthrope attempts to kill themself, but fails.
tips: You know two good players — share this information carefully. They are valuable allies, but one is unreliable.
Try to figure out which of your two known players is behaving strangely or giving info that doesn't match the game state. That's likely your drunk.
Don't reveal both names at once. If evil learns who your pair is, they know neither can fully be trusted and may use that to confuse the town.
Even after you die, the drunk persists. Consider whether revealing your pair on your deathbed helps or hurts the town.
firstNightReminder: Point to 2 good players. :reminder: :reminder:
callout: If the Waiter learns false info due to drunkenness, poisoning or an ability such as the Vortox forcing false info, they may either learn 2 evil players or 1 good and 1 evil player. Either way, one of the players the Waiter learned becomes drunk when the Waiter becomes sober and healthy. It is not recommended to make an evil player drunk this way.

name: Singer
ability: Once per game, during the day, publicly choose any volunteers. If a Demon is chosen, you die tonight.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: A singsong girl, unaware of the sorrow of a conquered state, is singing Backyard Flowers on the river's other shore.
lede: The Singer invites players to a performance, but risks exposing themself to the Demon.
summaryBullets: The Singer can choose to use their ability at any time during the day, and must declare to everyone when they're using it.

When the Singer declares their ability, all players (alive or dead) are asked to raise their hands if they would like to attend. The Singer may choose any number of players who have their hands raised. All players know whom the Singer has chosen.
If the Demon had their hand raised and was chosen by the Singer, the Singer dies tonight.
If a non-Demon player chosen by the Singer becomes a Demon later, the Singer does not die due to their own ability tonight.
If the Singer was drunk or poisoned when they chose the Demon today, they still die if they are sober and healthy tonight.
A Singer that uses their ability while poisoned or drunk may not use it again.
howToRun: Once per game, during the day, the Singer can declare that they wish to use their ability. If so, all players are told to raise their hands if they want to be chosen by the Singer. Count down from 3 to 1 to let players know how long they have to decide. When the time is out, ask the Singer to choose any players who have their hand raised.
If the Demon is chosen, mark the Singer with the [[DEAD]] reminder token. Mark the Singer with the [[NO ABILITY]] reminder token.
Tonight, if the Singer is marked with the [[DEAD]] reminder token, they die.
examples: The Singer reveals themself and invites the group to a performance. 5 players raise their hands. The Singer selects the alive Tinker, the alive Pit-Hag and the dead Fang Gu. The Singer dies tonight.
tips: Watch who does NOT volunteer. The Demon may avoid stepping forward to dodge the risk — an absence can be as telling as a presence.
Conversely, a confident Demon might volunteer knowing they're well-hidden. Don't assume a volunteer is safe.
Save your ability for a moment when you're confident about a specific player — it only fires once.
If you volunteer as a non-Demon player, you're helping the Singer gather information. Consider whether you want to be in their candidate pool.
otherNightReminder: If the Singer is marked with the DEAD reminder, they die.

name: Abbot
ability: Each night, the 1st time an evil ability begins affecting one of your alive neighbors, it fails: you learn when this happens.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Always polish the mirror of the mind, so that no dust can collect.
lede: The Abbot shelters their neighbors indiscriminately.
summaryBullets: The Abbot's ability works once per night.
If an evil player's ability would affect one of the Abbot's alive neighbours, it fails. If they act multiple times, only the time they target the Abbot's neighbor fails.
The Abbot only causes evil players' abilities to fail during the night. Abilities that take effect during the day still happen as normal.
The Abbot only protects from abilities that have not yet activated. If an Abbot's living neighbor dies, and the next living neighbor was previously poisoned, the Abbot cannot remove the poisoning.

When the Abbot's ability activates, the Storyteller wakes the Abbot. The Abbot does not learn which player's or character's ability they prevented, nor which neighbor they protected. They only learn that their ability activated.
If the Abbot cannot protect their neighbors from an evil player's ability, the Abbot's ability does not activate and they do not wake up.
howToRun: At night, if an evil player uses their ability to target one of the Abbot's living neighbors, or if an evil player's ability would passively begin affecting one of the Abbot's living neighbors, it fails: treat the evil player as if they are drunk in this instance. Place the Abbot's [[PACIFIED]] reminder token next to the Abbot. Then, wake the Abbot and show them the Abbot character token. Put the Abbot to sleep.
If one of the Abbot's neighbors would be targeted or affected by an evil player's ability and the Abbot's [[PACIFIED]] token is placed, resolve the evil player's ability as normal.
At dawn, remove the Abbot's [[PACIFIED]] reminder token. If the pacified ability has a continuous effect, it takes effect again once the [[PACIFIED]] token is removed.
examples: The Imp kills themself at night. The Baron is the closest living neighbor to the Abbot, and thus cannot be affected by the Imp's ability. As there is no living Demon, the Storyteller announces that the game ends and the good team wins.
The Assassin chooses to attack the Fool, who is neighboring the Abbot. The Fool dies, and the Abbot does not wake.

The Philosopher is sitting between the Abbot and the Snake Charmer, whose other neighbor is the Town Crier. The Snake Charmer chooses a player who is the No Dashii and becomes the evil No Dashii. The Philosopher and the Town Crier are about to be poisoned, but since the Philosopher is sitting next to the Abbot, the No Dashii's poisoning fails, and neither are poisoned. On the next day, the No Dashii poisons the Philosopher and the Town Crier, as the Abbot's ability is not active during the day.
tips: Sitting next to powerful information roles makes you extremely valuable. Evil will be reluctant to attack your neighbors — or will waste attempts trying.
Your protection is reactive, not announced — evil doesn't know you've blocked them until the player survives. Use this surprise to track which nights evil tried to act near you.
Tell trusted players near you that they have some protection. They might take bolder actions knowing there's a safety net.
Be aware that you only stop the first evil ability per night. If two evil abilities target your neighbors, only one is blocked — plan accordingly.

jinxes.reason: If the Leviathan nominates and executes one of the Abbot’s alive neighbors, that player does not die. That night, the Abbot learns that their ability has activated, and their ability cannot activate again tonight.
jinxes.name: Leviathan

jinxes.reason: The Kazali can choose that a player neighboring the Abbot is one of their evil Minions.
jinxes.name: Kazali

jinxes.reason: If the Riot nominates and executes one of the Abbot’s alive neighbors, that player does not die. That night, the Abbot learns that their ability has activated, and their ability cannot activate again tonight.
jinxes.name: Riot

callout: If the No Dashii is poisoning a Townsfolk neighbor of the Abbot at the start of the game, the Abbot's ability does not activate. If the No Dashii begins poisoning a neighbor of the Abbot mid-game, the Abbot will prevent the No Dashii's poisoning until dawn. Abilities that poison all good players or Townsfolk, such as the Xaan or Hun Dun, also poison the Abbot.

name: Brocadier
ability: Each night*, choose a player: until dusk, if they die, you die instead.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Come hell or high water, I will protect you, my liege!
lede: The Brocadier protects other players with their life.
summaryBullets: Each night except the first, the Brocadier chooses a player. If the chosen player is about to die, they live and the Brocadier dies instead.
The dead Brocadier loses their ability and cannot protect the chosen player again the same night. The Brocadier can protect a player only once.
If the chosen player is about to die but is also protected by other abilities, the Brocadier's ability has no effect, and the Brocadier does not die.
If the Brocadier cannot die for some reason, they cannot protect the chosen player from dying.
The Brocadier's protection lasts during the day. The chosen player can survive execution, exile and anything else. The Brocadier will die instead.
If the Brocadier successfully protects a player, they die due to their own ability, not due to what would have killed the protected player.
The Brocadier can choose dead players, but since a dead player cannot die again, the Brocadier's ability will have no effect in this case.
howToRun: Each night except the first, wake the Brocadier. They point to a player. Mark that player with the [[PROTECTED]] reminder token. Put the Brocadier to sleep.

If the player marked with the Brocadier's [[PROTECTED]] reminder token is about to die, and the Brocadier can protect them from death, the marked player does not die. If this happens during the day, declare that the Brocadier dies, and remove the [[PROTECTED]] reminder token. If this happens at night, place the [[DEAD]] reminder token by the Brocadier's character token, and remove the [[PROTECTED]] reminder token. At dawn, declare that the Brocadier died in the night.
At dusk, remove the [[PROTECTED]] reminder token if it is still placed.
examples: The Brocadier chooses the Imp. The Imp chooses to kill themself, but survives, and no Minion becomes the Imp. The Brocadier dies.
The Brocadier chooses the Balloonist. The next day, the Psychopath chooses to attack the Balloonist. The Balloonist survives, and the Brocadier dies.
tips: Protect players whose abilities are still needed — a Fortune Teller in the late game or a Slayer who hasn't fired are prime candidates.
Evil knows you exist if you survive after your protected player should have died. Use this carefully — your survival tells the town someone was protected.
Consider protecting yourself on nights when you feel particularly targeted. You are valuable as long as you can keep protecting others.
Coordinate with your protected player if you trust them — they can take risks knowing you have their back tonight.
otherNightReminder: The Brocadier points to a player. :reminder:
callout: If the Fang Gu is protected by the Brocadier the first time an Outsider dies to its ability, both the Outsider and the Brocadier die, and the Fang Gu survives.

name: Herb Doctor
ability: Each night, choose a player (not yourself): you learn a word related to their ability.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Increased heart rate. Your qi is weak, dispersing.
lede: The Herb Doctor can infer things about players' capabilities.
summaryBullets: Each night, the Herb Doctor chooses a player. They learn a word related to that player's ability. The Storyteller can write the word on a piece of paper or a phone or other device, and show it to the Herb Doctor.
The word could be a word that appears in the chosen player's ability text, such as "mad", "night" or "die". It can also be a word describing the ability, such as "protect", "support" or "recurring".
The Herb Doctor generally only learns one word at a time. The word should be meaningful, not a function word such as "is" or "on".
The Herb Doctor can choose the same player each night, and can choose dead players. The Herb Doctor cannot choose themself.
howToRun: Each night, wake the Herb Doctor. They point to any player. Write a word referring to that player's character ability and show it to the Herb Doctor. Put the Herb Doctor to sleep.
examples: The Herb Doctor chooses the Damsel and learns the word "public". Next night, they choose the Juggler and learn the word "public".
The Herb Doctor chooses the Bounty Hunter and learns the word "evil", since the Bounty Hunter has the word "evil" in their name.
The Herb Doctor chooses the Lycanthrope and learns the word "sacrifice". Next night, they choose the Soldier and learn the word "brave".
tips: Cross-reference the word you receive with the script. If the word narrows it down to two or three characters, you have powerful deduction material.
Target players you already have suspicions about — a word that confirms or denies your theory is more valuable than information about a known-safe player.
Be careful about revealing your information publicly early. Evil players know what word their own ability would generate — they might bluff around it.
If you receive the same word for two different players, they likely share an ability mechanic. This can help cluster your deductions.
firstNightReminder: The Herb Doctor points to a player. Write down a word relating to that player’s ability and show it to the Herb Doctor.
otherNightReminder: The Herb Doctor points to a player. Write down a word relating to that player’s ability and show it to the Herb Doctor.
callout: In the early game, you can give the Herb Doctor more ambiguous words so they don't immediately guess the character of the chosen player. If the good team is at a disadvantage, you can give the Herb Doctor more unambiguous words.

name: Stargazer
ability: You start knowing if your closest evil player is clockwise, anti-clockwise or equal. If they neighbor you, this info is false.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Strange phenomena in the heavens. Something wicked approaches.
lede: The Stargazer can see evil in the stars, but is blinded if they're too close.
summaryBullets: The closest evil player is the player the smallest number of steps from the Stargazer to the evil player.
If the evil players are equidistant, the Storyteller shows this by pointing down instead of to the side.

If either of the Stargazer's two neighbors is evil, the Stargazer learns false info. If one neighbor is evil, the Stargazer learns the opposite direction or equidistant. If both are evil, the Stargazer cannot learn that they are equidistant.
The Stargazer does not learn the number of steps from them to the closest evil player.
If the Stargazer is created mid-game, they learn their information that night. Dead players are included in the count.
howToRun: On the first night, wake the Stargazer. Point your finger horizontally in the direction of the closest evil player to the Stargazer, or point your finger downwards if the evil players on both sides are equidistant to the Stargazer. If the Stargazer neighbors an evil player, give false info. Put the Stargazer to sleep.
examples: The Stargazer's clockwise neighbors are the Klutz, then the Psychopath. The Stargazer's anti-clockwise neighbors are the Juggler, then the good Judge, then the Po. The Stargazer learns "clockwise".

The Stargazer is created mid-game. The Stargazer's clockwise neighbor is the dead Marionette, and their anti-clockwise neighbor is the alive Fang Gu. The Stargazer can learn either "clockwise" or "anti-clockwise", but not "equidistant", since that is correct info.
tips: Your information tells you a direction, not a specific player — use it to narrow your suspicions to a half of the circle.
If your immediate neighbor is evil, your info is false — the fact that it seems to point away from your neighbors might itself be a clue.
Share your information with players who have complementary knowledge. A Fortune Teller working the same side of the circle as your info points can triangulate faster.
Your info is fixed from night one and doesn't update — late game deaths don't change which direction evil started in.
firstNightReminder: Point clockwise or anticlockwise, or down if equidistant.

name: Enlightened One
ability: You think you are an Outsider. If affected by an evil player’s ability, you first become a not-in-play Townsfolk.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: The Six Paths are trees obscuring the forest; in truth, all beings are equal.
lede: The Enlightened One realizes that they can't sit back when evil is upon them.
summaryBullets: During setup, the Enlightened One's token does not go in the bag. Instead, an Outsider character token goes in the bag, and the player who draws that token is secretly the Enlightened One.
The Enlightened One does not have the Outsider ability they think they have.
If the Enlightened One is targeted or affected by an evil player's ability, they will change characters and become a not-in-play Townsfolk character before the evil player's ability takes effect.
If the Enlightened One changes characters during the day, they learn of their new character immediately at the start of the night.
When the Enlightened One is affected by an evil ability, they become the new character and are no longer the Enlightened One.
howToRun: While setting up the game, before putting character tokens in the bag, remove the Enlightened One token and add an Outsider character token. Add the Enlightened One's [[IS THE ENLIGHTENED ONE]] reminder token to the Grimoire. Put the swapped Outsider character token in the bag, not the Enlightened One character token.

While preparing the first night, put the [[IS THE ENLIGHTENED ONE]] reminder token by any Outsider character token, changing that player's character to the Enlightened One. They are now a Townsfolk, and do not have the ability of the Outsider character they drew.
During the game, act as if the Enlightened One is actually the Outsider character.

If the Enlightened One is targeted or affected by an evil player's ability, remove the [[IS THE ENLIGHTENED ONE]] reminder token and replace their character token with a not-in-play Townsfolk character token. If it happens at night, immediately wake the Enlightened One, show them the YOU ARE info token, then the Enlightened One character token and the token of their new Townsfolk character. Put them to sleep. If it happens during the day, inform them of their character change at the beginning of the night.
examples: The Enlightened One believes they are the Damsel, but no Minion learns that the Damsel is in play. At night, the Pit-Hag chooses the Enlightened One to become the Tinker. The Enlightened One learns that they are the Tinker now, and does not learn that they used to be the Enlightened One.

The Enlightened One believes they are the Heretic, and accidentally shares this with the Demon. That night, the Demon chooses to kill themself. The Storyteller announces that the game ends and that the good team won, since the Enlightened One does not have the Heretic's ability.
tips: You genuinely believe you are an Outsider — play your claimed role as if it's real. Your transformation is a surprise to you as much as anyone.
If you transform, pay close attention to what triggered it — that means an evil player targeted you. You may have narrowed down who evil is.
Your new Townsfolk identity may grant you a powerful ability. Use it immediately and transparently — the town needs to know the situation changed.
Telling the town you believe you're an Outsider is risky: evil knows what you are and may avoid targeting you to prevent your transformation.

jinxes.reason: If there is a spare token, the Enlightened One can become an extra Village Idiot. If so, the drunk Village Idiot might change.
jinxes.name: Village Idiot

jinxes.reason: The Demon cannot have the Enlightened One ability.
jinxes.name: Boffin

callout: The Enlightened One believes themself to be an Outsider. For this reason, they cannot believe themself to be the Drunk or the Lunatic, since those characters don't know that they are Outsiders at the beginning of the game.

name: Rascal
ability: Each night, choose a Townsfolk character: their ability yields false info until dusk.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: This one's mine! This one, too!
lede: The Rascal is mischievous and confuses other Townsfolk.
summaryBullets: Each night, the Rascal has to choose a Townsfolk character. If they are in play, the player with that character will learn false info until dusk, and will give false info to other players.
If the chosen player is drunk or poisoned, they have no ability and cannot give false info to other players, but they still learn false info from the ability they think they have.
The chosen player still learns correct info from other abilities and basic rules, such as what their alignment is and what character they are.
The Rascal only affects the player who is the chosen character. They do not affect players who have the chosen ability without being the character.
If the Rascal chooses an out-of-play character that then enters play, they are still affected by the Rascal's ability.
howToRun: Each night, wake the Rascal. They point to any Townsfolk character on their character sheet. Put the Rascal to sleep.
If the chosen character is in play, mark the player of the chosen character with the [[FALSE INFO]] reminder token.
If a player with the [[FALSE INFO]] reminder token uses their ability to yield info, provide false info.
At dusk, remove the [[FALSE INFO]] reminder token.
examples: The Rascal chooses the Empath, who neighbors 2 evil players. Tonight, the Empath learns 0.
The Rascal chooses the Flowergirl. The Philosopher chooses to gain the Flowergirl's ability and learns correct info. The now drunk Flowergirl learns false info.
tips: Target the most trusted information role in play — if the town is building their case around a Fortune Teller's reads, corrupting those reads derails everything.
Vary your targets. If you always corrupt the same player, the town may notice a pattern of unreliable information from one source.

You can corrupt your own cover story: choose a character you're claiming to be, making your 'ability result' wrong — this can paradoxically make you seem more trustworthy since 'your' info is also wrong.
Coordinate with your Demon about who to corrupt — the Demon knows who to kill, you know whose information to invalidate the same night.
firstNightReminder: The Rascal points to any Townsfolk character on their character sheet. :reminder:
otherNightReminder: The Rascal points to any Townsfolk character on their character sheet. :reminder:

name: Yinyang Diviner
ability: You start knowing 2 good & 2 evil characters, exactly 2 of which are in play.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: The great Xuanwu is before us. Gods return to their temples, ghosts return to their graves, monsters return to the mountains and forests!
lede: The Yinyang Diviner learns 4 characters, but not which of them are in play.
summaryBullets: The Storyteller decides which in-play characters should be shown to the Yinyang Diviner. It could be 1 good and 1 evil, 2 good or 2 evil characters.
Characters such as the Recluse or the Spy can register as a different character to disrupt the Yinyang Diviner's info.
If a Yinyang Diviner enters play mid-game, they learn their info that night instead.
howToRun: During setup, decide which characters to show to the Yinyang Diviner: two good characters and two evil characters, any two of which are in play. Mark the in-play character tokens with the Yinyang Diviner's [[LEARNED]] reminder tokens. Keep the two not-in-play character tokens available.
During the first night, wake the Yinyang Diviner and show them the four character tokens. Put the Yinyang Diviner to sleep.
examples: The Yinyang Diviner learns the Fortune Teller, the Goon, the Cerenovus and the Lleech. Only the Goon and the Cerenovus are in play.

The Yinyang Diviner learns the Soldier, the Undertaker, the Witch and the Mezepheles. None of these characters are in play; the Recluse is registering as the Witch and the Spy is registering as the Undertaker using their abilities.

The Pit-Hag creates a Yinyang Diviner mid-game. The Fang Gu attacks the Mutant and dies, turning the Mutant into the new Fang Gu. The Yinyang Diviner learns the Clockmaker, the Mutant, the Pit-Hag and the Fang Gu. Since the Pit-Hag and the Fang Gu are in play, and there is no longer a Mutant in play, this info is correct.
tips: You know one evil character that exists in the game — this is powerful even if you can't pinpoint the player. Share your evil options with the town.
Combine your information with other players' claims. If someone claims to be a character from your 'not real' group, they're either lying or you received false info.
Your good pair also helps: if one of your good characters is claimed by someone and their play seems consistent, you've found a trustworthy ally — and narrowed your evil candidates.
Be aware that your info is first-night only. Character changes (Pit-Hag, Barber) may make your information stale by mid-game.
firstNightReminder: Show the Yinyang Diviner the 4 character tokens.
callout: If the Yinyang Diviner must learn false info, they still learn 2 good and 2 evil characters, but cannot learn exactly 2 in-play characters.

name: Turncoat
ability: On your 1st night, choose another player. The 1st time one of you dies by execution, the other turns evil that night.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: I fear his hesitation will cost the general this unique opportunity. A pity.
lede: The Turncoat is at odds with another player.
summaryBullets: On the first night, the Turncoat chooses another player. When one of them dies by execution, the other turns evil that night.
If either player dies for any other reason than execution, the other player does not turn evil.
If the Turncoat dies from something other than execution, they lose their ability. If the chosen player later dies by execution, the Turncoat will not change alignment.
If the Turncoat chose an evil player, that player will not become good if the Turncoat dies by execution.
howToRun: The first night, wake the Turncoat. They point to another player. Mark the chosen player with the [[RIVAL]] reminder token. Put the Turncoat to sleep.
If the Turncoat is the first of the two to die by execution, the player marked with the [[RIVAL]] reminder token turns evil tonight.
If the [[RIVAL]] player is the first of the two to die by execution, the Turncoat turns evil tonight.

Once a player turns evil, wake them and show them the YOU ARE info token, followed by a thumbs down for evil. Put them to sleep. Turn their character token 180 degrees in the Grimoire and remove the [[RIVAL]] reminder token.
examples: The Turncoat chooses the Snake Charmer. On the first day, the Snake Charmer dies by execution. The Turncoat turns evil that night.
The Turncoat chooses the Pit-Hag. On the next day, the Turncoat dies by execution. Nothing happens, since the Pit-Hag is already evil.
The Turncoat chooses the Philosopher, who is then killed by the Demon at night. The next day, the Turncoat dies by execution. That night, the dead Philosopher turns evil.
tips: You have an incentive to avoid being executed — and to protect your Rival from execution too. This creates a genuine conflict of interest with the town.
Consider who you choose as Rival carefully. Choosing a likely-to-be-executed Minion means you'll turn evil on a Minion execution — potentially very soon.
If you turn evil, you gain your new alignment immediately but the town doesn't know it. Use your insider status carefully.
As a good player who doesn't know about Turncoat: if an execution suddenly seems to benefit evil more than expected, consider whether there's a Turncoat in play.
firstNightReminder: The Turncoat points to another player. Mark that player with the RIVAL reminder token. :reminder:
otherNightReminder: The first time the Turncoat or the player marked RIVAL dies by execution, wake the other one, show them the YOU ARE info token and show a thumbs down for evil.

name: Lummox
ability: If you die, 1 alive neighbor might die tonight.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Born under an unlucky star? Nonsense! I can't be faulted for the misfortunes of people around me!
lede: The Lummox brings bad luck to their neighbors.
summaryBullets: When the Lummox dies, the Storyteller decides if their ability should activate.
If the Storyteller decides that the Lummox's ability activates, an alive neighbor of the Lummox dies that night.
If the Lummox is poisoned or drunk when they die, or a dead player becomes the Lummox, nothing happens.
howToRun: When the Lummox dies, decide whether to activate their ability. If you do, mark one of their alive neighbors with the Lummox's [[DEAD]] reminder token. At dawn, declare that the player marked [[DEAD]] died in the night.
examples: The Demon attacks the Lummox, who dies. The Storyteller decides not to activate the Lummox's ability, and their alive neighbors do not die that night.
The Moonchild dies by execution and chooses the Lummox. Tonight, the Lummox dies, and so does the Baron, who is sitting next to the Lummox.
tips: Seat yourself next to players you suspect are evil. If they kill you, they might accidentally kill another evil player.
Revealing yourself as a Lummox can deter executions — the town won't want to risk losing a good neighbor.
Conversely, don't reveal too early. If evil knows about you they'll be careful about killing you near their Minions.
If you die and no neighbor dies, that's a clue — either the Storyteller chose mercy or your neighbors were valuable to keep alive.
otherNightReminder: If the Lummox died today, one of their living neighbors might die. :reminder:

name: Ambassador
ability: When an evil player is chosen by an ability at night, you might be targeted instead (once per night), even if dead.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: The only thing worse than travelling alone in the wilderness is the company one might find there.
lede: The Ambassador travels foreign lands and is often harassed by the locals.
summaryBullets: The Ambassador's ability can activate no more than once per night. It may activate each night, or only some nights. The Storyteller decides whether to activate their ability or not.
If a player uses their ability to actively choose another player, and if that player is evil, the ability may be redirected to the Ambassador.
Abilities that do not involve a choice cannot be redirected by the Ambassador's ability.
howToRun: Each night, if an evil player is targeted by an ability and the Ambassador's [[TARGETED TONIGHT]] reminder token is not placed, you can choose to activate the Ambassador's ability. If you do, resolve the ability as if the Ambassador was chosen instead of the evil player, and place the Ambassador's [[TARGETED TONIGHT]] token next to the Ambassador character token.
At dawn, remove the [[TARGETED TONIGHT]] reminder token if it is placed in the Grimoire.
examples: The Fortune Teller chooses the Seamstress and the Riot. The Storyteller decides that the Ambassador's ability activates, making the Fortune Teller instead target the Seamstress and the Ambassador. The Fortune Teller learns "no".

The Pit-Hag chooses to turn an evil Turncoat into the Vigormortis. The Storyteller decides that the Ambassador's ability does not activate. Later, the Cerenovus chooses themself and the Sage. The Storyteller decides to activate the Ambassador's ability, and the Ambassador learns that the Cerenovus has chosen them to be "mad" that they are the Sage, or risk execution. The Cerenovus learns nothing.
tips: Your ability means evil players have some protection from targeting abilities — but it also means information about evil gets muddied. This is double-edged.
If an information role gets a reading that seems wildly off, consider whether the Ambassador redirected their action.
Being dead doesn't stop you — the Ambassador is one of the few characters whose ability persists usefully after death.
Coordinate with your team: if you know you've been redirected (because an info role got wrong data), that means someone targeted an evil player that night.

jinxes.reason: The Lleech’s poisoning or host choice cannot be redirected.
jinxes.name: Lleech

name: Pedant
ability: The Demon knows you’re in play. If they privately guess you (once): tonight, they may choose a player to die (even if you die).
creator: Gstone Games
quote: I accept my fate, but first tell me: at whose hands am I dying?
lede: The Pedant must hide from the Demon, or bring their teammates down with them.
summaryBullets: The Demon starts knowing that the Pedant is in play.

At any time during the day, the Demon may privately speak to the Storyteller to guess who the Pedant is. If the Demon guesses correctly, they are awoken to kill a player that night, even if the Pedant dies after the correct guess.
The chosen player dies due to the Pedant's ability, not the Demon's. Therefore, characters such as the Soldier are not safe from this death.
If there is more than one Demon player, the Demons share one chance to make a correct guess.
howToRun: During the first night, wake the Demon and show them the Pedant character token. Put the Demon to sleep.

During the day, the Demon may visit you in private at any time to guess which player has the Pedant ability. If they guess correctly and no Demon has already made a guess, put the [[GUESS USED]] reminder token next to the Demon player's character token. If they guess wrong, put the [[GUESS USED]] reminder token next to the Pedant character token.

During the night, if the [[GUESS USED]] reminder token is placed next to a Demon player's character token, after the Demon has used their ability, show them the Pedant character token. They shake their head no or point to a player. If they point to a player, that player dies. Mark that player with the Pedant's [[DEAD]] reminder token. Move the Pedant's [[GUESS USED]] reminder token to the Pedant's character token.
At dawn, declare who died during the night.
examples: The No Dashii correctly guesses who the Pedant is. Tonight, the No Dashii chooses to kill the Poppy Grower, and then the Sage. The Sage does not wake to use their ability, as they didn't die to the No Dashii's ability.

The Demon correctly guesses who the Pedant is. Tonight, the Philosopher chooses to gain the Pedant ability, making the Pedant drunk. However, since the Pedant ability is in play and the Demon made a correct guess, the Demon can still choose to kill a player using the Pedant ability.
The Demon correctly guesses who the Pedant is. Tonight, the Courtier chooses the Pedant, who becomes drunk. The Demon does not get to use the Pedant ability, and thinks they guessed wrong.
tips: Don't reveal that you're the Pedant early — the Demon is trying to find you. Once they do, they get a free kill.
If two players die in one night and there's no obvious explanation, the Demon may have found the Pedant.
As a Pedant, behave unpredictably. Don't make yourself obviously stand out — the Demon is watching for tells.
Misdirecting the Demon about who the Pedant is (e.g., someone claiming to be Pedant to absorb the guess) can waste the Demon's one guess.
firstNightReminder: Show the Demon the Pedant character token.
otherNightReminder: If the Demon guessed the Pedant player correctly, wake the Demon and show them the Pedant token. They may point to any player: that player DIES. :reminder:

name: Bug Keeper
ability: Each night*, if you were alive when nominated today, your nominator dies, even if you die first.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: The venom of the centipede, who ate the spider, who ate the scorpion, who ate the snake… No, it's not enough!
lede: The Bug Keeper retaliates against their enemies.
summaryBullets: If a living Bug Keeper is nominated during the day, the player who nominated them dies that night, even if the Bug Keeper died by execution.
If the living Bug Keeper was nominated by another evil player, that player still dies that night.
If the Bug Keeper is dead when nominated, the nominator does not die that night.
howToRun: If the Bug Keeper is alive and a player nominates them during the day, mark that player with the [[DEAD]] reminder token.
Tonight, any players with the [[DEAD]] reminder token die, unless they are protected. Put a shroud on their character token and remove the [[DEAD]] reminder token.
examples: On the first day, the Juggler uses their ability and then nominates the Bug Keeper. Tonight, the Juggler dies without waking up or learning their info.
The Boomdandy nominates the Bug Keeper, who is executed and dies. That night, the Boomdandy dies.
On the second night, the Vigormortis kills the Bug Keeper. The third day, the dead Bug Keeper is nominated. The nominator does not die, as the Bug Keeper was not alive when nominated.
tips: Let it be known you're the Bug Keeper early — it will deter nominations. The town won't want to lose a player just for nominating you.
If you're confident enough about a Demon, consider letting yourself be nominated to bait the nominator into dying — especially if the nominator is suspicious.
Be careful: revealing your identity means evil knows not to nominate you. They'll find other ways to deal with you.
If a player dies the night after nominating someone and there's no obvious cause, the Bug Keeper may be in play.
otherNightReminder: Any player who nominated the alive Bug Keeper dies.

jinxes.reason: If the Storyteller would gain the Bug Keeper ability, a living Minion gains the Bug Keeper ability in addition to their own ability, and learns this.
jinxes.name: Plague Doctor

callout: Abilities that cause the Bug Keeper to be nominated more than once, such as the Butcher, can cause more than one player to die by the Bug Keeper's ability in one night.

name: Vixen
ability: On your 1st night, choose a player: they learn the Vixen is in play. If you die by execution, they become evil tonight.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Your Majesty, the concubine had two sisters. They send their condolences and have requested an audience.
lede: The Vixen charms a player to join the evil side if the Vixen dies.
summaryBullets: During the first night, the Vixen chooses a player. They learn that the Vixen is in play.
If the Vixen is drunk or poisoned when they make their choice, the chosen player learns the Vixen is in play once the Vixen becomes sober and healthy.
If the Vixen dies by execution, the player they chose turns evil that night.
If the player the Vixen chose is already evil when the Vixen dies by execution, nothing happens.
howToRun: During the first night, wake the Vixen. They point to any player. Mark that player with the [[CHARMED]] reminder token and put the Vixen to sleep. Wake the chosen player. Show them the THIS CHARACTER SELECTED YOU info token, followed by the Vixen character token. Put them to sleep.

When the Vixen dies by execution, turn the character token of the player marked [[CHARMED]] upside down. That night, wake the player marked [[CHARMED]] and show them the YOU ARE info token, followed by thumbs down for evil. If they were already evil, nothing happens.
examples: On the first night, the Vixen chooses the Philosopher. The Philosopher learns that the Vixen is in play. On the first day, the Vixen dies by execution. That night, the Philosopher turns evil, and learns this.
The Vixen was drunk the first night, when they chose the Alchemist. The Alchemist does not learn that the Vixen is in play until the second night, when the Vixen is sober.
The Minstrel is in play. When the Vixen dies by execution, everyone is drunk. As the Vixen is drunk that night, their chosen player does not turn evil.
tips: Charm a powerful information role — if they turn evil, that's devastating to the good team's information network.
Let evil know who you've charmed, so they know who to protect you with — keeping you alive keeps your Charmed ally safe.
The Charmed player knows someone is the Vixen. They might deduce it's you. Bluff carefully.
If you're the Charmed player, you know someone is the Vixen — but you don't know who. Be careful about pushing too hard to execute someone who might be the Vixen.
firstNightReminder: The Vixen points to a player. :reminder: Put the Vixen to sleep and wake the chosen player. Show them the THIS CHARACTER SELECTED YOU info token and the Vixen character token. Put them to sleep.
otherNightReminder: If the Vixen died by execution today, turn the character token marked CHARMED upside-down. Wake that player, show them the YOU ARE info token, and thumbs down for evil.

jinxes.reason: Only 1 jinxed character can be in play.
jinxes.name: Heretic

jinxes.reason: If the Storyteller would gain the Vixen ability, a living Minion gains the Vixen ability in addition to their own ability, and learns this.
jinxes.name: Plague Doctor

name: Corpse Walker
ability: The 1st time each of your Townsfolk neighbors die, they register as alive. [-1 Outsider]
creator: Gstone Games
quote: Only journey under the cover of darkness, and never let them find out that your stomach is full of straw.
lede: The Corpse Walker secretly turns their Townsfolk neighbors undead.
summaryBullets: The first time a Townsfolk player neighboring the Corpse Walker dies, they turn undead and appear to survive. Their character tokens are shrouded in the Grimoire, but their life tokens on the town square are not flipped.
Undead players register as alive to other players' abilities.
Undead players are dead and have no ability. They are treated as if they are drunk or poisoned, waking as normal during the night but learning arbitrary info.
Abilities that activate upon death do not activate upon undeath, since undead players register as alive.
If an undead player "dies" again, their death is announced. They cease to be undead and register as dead from then on.
howToRun: While setting up the game, before putting the character tokens in the bag, remove one Outsider character token and add one Townsfolk character token.

The first time each Townsfolk neighbor of the Corpse Walker dies, they turn undead. Place the Corpse Walker's [[UNDEAD]] reminder token next to the dead player's character token. Put a shroud on their character token in the Grimoire, but do not declare that they die and do not flip over their life token in the town square. If they died by execution, declare that they survived.
If an undead player dies again, declare that they died and flip over their life token in the town square.
examples: The Sage, the Butler, the Corpse Walker and the Innkeeper are sitting in a line. Tonight, the Demon attacks the Sage. Since the Sage is a Townsfolk neighbor of the Corpse Walker, they turn undead and lose their ability. The Sage does not wake tonight, and their death is not announced. The next night, the Sage is attacked by the Demon again. They do not wake up, but their death is announced at dawn.

There are four players left alive: the Rascal, the Imp, the Corpse Walker and the Tea Lady. However, the Tea Lady is actually undead and only registers as alive because of the Corpse Walker's ability. The town decides not to execute anyone. That night, the Imp kills the Rascal. The evil team has won, as there are only two living players left.
tips: Your undead neighbors create confusion about who is alive and dead — use this to mislead evil about when the final day is approaching.
Sit next to powerful roles. When they die, their continued 'alive' registration may protect others from deducing who was killed.
Be aware that the [-1 Outsider] setup change makes you easier to find — the Outsider count will be off by one.
If you can keep your identity secret, evil won't know why their kills aren't showing up in the living count.

jinxes.reason: If the Storyteller would gain the Corpse Walker ability, a living Minion gains the Corpse Walker ability in addition to their own ability, and learns this.
jinxes.name: Plague Doctor

callout: It is not recommended to have more than two undead players at a time, since this can make the game end unexpectedly early.

name: Imperialist
ability: Players who are mad that they want to live or die (you start by choosing which) might be immediately executed.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: He deserved to be beheaded! Yes, he broke curfew, my lord!
lede: The Imperialist provides a little "help" to those who want to die… or to those who don't.
summaryBullets: The first night, the Imperialist chooses whether to target players who want to die or players who want to live.
If a player says that they are okay with dying or living, they might be immediately executed in accordance with the Imperialist's choice.
It is up to the Storyteller to decide whether a player has been sufficiently "mad" that they want to live or die.
If a player is "mad" that they want to live or die during the night, the Storyteller may choose to execute them at night. This does not count towards the maximum of one execution per day.
The execution happens immediately.
howToRun: During the first night, wake the Imperialist and show them the [[LIVE]] and [[DIE]] reminder tokens. They point to one. Place that reminder token next to the Imperialist character token in the Grimoire. Put the Imperialist to sleep.

During the game, if you believe that a player is "mad" that they want to live or die, depending on which the Imperialist chose, you can decide to execute them. Declare to the group that this player is executed (do not say why). If this happens during the day, the day ends.
examples: The Imperialist has chosen "live". Today, the town nominates the Recluse, who says that they do not mind dying. The Recluse does not gain enough votes, and is not executed.

The Imperialist has chosen "die". The good Gambler and the good Gossip are sitting next to the Tea Lady. Today, the Gossip asks to be nominated and executed. The Storyteller immediately declares that the Gossip is executed. The Gossip survives thanks to the Tea Lady, and the day ends.
The Imperialist has chosen "die". At night, the Soldier asks the Demon to attack them. The Storyteller declares that the Soldier is executed. The Soldier is declared dead at dawn.
tips: Your ability creates paranoia about expressing preferences publicly. Once the town knows an Imperialist might be in play, players become reluctant to advocate strongly for their own survival.
Choose 'LIVE' if you want to punish players who desperately defend themselves during nominations.
Keep your choice secret if possible — if the town knows which way you chose, they'll simply avoid performing the relevant madness.
Coordinate with the Demon: if a key good player is being diplomatic, getting them to strongly advocate for living might get them executed.
firstNightReminder: Show the Imperialist the LIVE and DIE reminder tokens: they point to one. Put the chosen token in the Grimoire. :reminder:

name: Tao Tie
ability: Each night*, choose up to 4 players (or 1 Traveller): if all are different character types, they die. [+1 Outsider]
creator: Gstone Games
quote: The beast looks like a ram with the face of a human, eyes under its armpits, tiger teeth and a baby's voice. It devours people, and its name is Tao Tie.
lede: The Tao Tie sometimes gets so ravenous it kills several players.
summaryBullets: Each night except the first, the Tao Tie can choose up to 4 non-Traveller players. If no two of them are the same character type, all chosen players die.
The Tao Tie can choose no one.
If the Tao Tie successfully kills more than one player, the chosen players die in the order they were chosen.
If the Tao Tie chooses a Traveller, they cannot choose any other player.
howToRun: While setting up the game, before putting character tokens in the bag, add one extra Outsider character token and remove one Townsfolk character token.

Each night except the first, wake the Tao Tie. They point at up to 4 players, or shake their head no. Put the Tao Tie to sleep. If no character type is represented more than once among the chosen players, all chosen players die in the order chosen—mark them with [[DEAD]] reminder tokens.
examples: The Tao Tie chooses the Fortune Teller, the Drunk and the Assassin. All 3 chosen players die.

The Tao Tie chooses the Undertaker, the good Goon and the Baron, and is the first to choose the Goon tonight. Since all 3 chosen players have different character types, the Undertaker dies, but the Tao Tie then becomes drunk due to the Goon's ability. The Goon turns evil, and both the Goon and the Baron survive.
tips: Planning a 4-player kill requires knowing one of each type. Focus your information gathering on identifying which type each player is.
Even a 2-player kill (one Townsfolk, one Minion) is extremely efficient — two types, two kills.
Outsiders are usually easy to identify since they often self-reveal. Use them as the anchor for your kill combination.
If you can't confirm types, a safe single-player kill keeps you hidden and avoids wasting a night on a failed 4-person attempt.
otherNightReminder: The Tao Tie shakes their head no, or points to up to 4 players. :reminder: :reminder: :reminder: :reminder:

name: Tao Wu
ability: Each night*, choose a player: they die. If you die, an alive Minion instead loses their ability. You learn no Demon info.
creator: Gstone Games
quote: The beast looks like a tiger with a human face, a mouth full of fangs and a tail of 18 foot. It destroys the land in its wake, and it is named Tao Wu.
lede: The Tao Wu draws strength from its Minions to extend its own life.
summaryBullets: If the Tao Wu is about to die, and a living Minion still has their ability, the Tao Wu does not die, and the Minion instead loses their ability. If no alive Minions have their ability, the Tao Wu dies.
If an alive Minion is drunk, poisoned, or has a No Ability reminder token, they have no ability to sacrifice for the Tao Wu.
The Tao Wu does not wake up for the Demon info step and does not learn who their Minions are or what characters are not in play.
howToRun: Do not wake the Tao Wu during the first night.
